content:
First note that the condition “\(n^2\) has the same remainder upon division by 7, 11, 13” means there is an integer \(r\) with
\[
0\le r\le6,\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod7,\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod{11},\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod{13}.
\]
Since \(\mathrm{lcm}(7,11,13)=1001\), this is equivalent to
\[
n^2\equiv r\pmod{1001}
\]
with \(0\le r\le6\).  We first count solutions in one full residue‐period \(n\pmod{1001}\).

For a prime \(p\), the number of solutions of \(x^2\equiv r\pmod p\) is
\[
\begin{cases}
1,&r\equiv0\pmod p,\\
2,&r\not\equiv0\pmod p\text{ and $r$ is a quadratic residue mod }p,\\
0,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
We tabulate for \(r=0,1,2,3,4,5,6\) the number of solutions mod 7, 11, 13:

\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccc}
r&0&1&2&3&4&5&6\\\hline
\#\{n^2\equiv r\pmod7\}&1&2&2&0&2&0&0\\
\#\{n^2\equiv r\pmod{11}\}&1&2&0&2&2&2&0\\
\#\{n^2\equiv r\pmod{13}\}&1&2&0&2&2&0&0
\end{array}
\]
Hence the total number of \(n\pmod{1001}\) with \(n^2\equiv r\) mod 7,11,13 simultaneously is
\[
\sum_{r=0}^6
\bigl[\#(\bmod7)\bigr]
\cdot\bigl[\#(\bmod11)\bigr]
\cdot\bigl[\#(\bmod13)\bigr]
=1\cdot1\cdot1
+2\cdot2\cdot2
+2\cdot2\cdot2
=1+8+8=17.
\]
Thus in each complete block of length 1001 there are 17 solutions.  Since \(1\le n<2018\) spans exactly two full blocks (giving \(2\cdot17=34\) solutions) plus the residues \(n\equiv1,2,\dots,15\pmod{1001}\).  A quick check shows that among \(1,2,\dots,15\) only \(n\equiv1\) and \(n\equiv2\) satisfy the condition.  Hence there are 2 more in the final partial block, for a grand total

\[
34+2=\boxed{36}.
\]

1,&r\equiv0\pmod p,\\
2,&r\not\equiv0\pmod p\text{ and $r$ is a quadratic residue mod }p,\\
0,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}

r&0&1&2&3&4&5&6\\\hline

\#\{n^2\equiv r\pmod{11}\}&1&2&0&2&2&2&0\\
